tweets.text:
Leopold Aschbrenner's last few years have been absolutely incredible. One day they'll probably make a movie out of this run. The fact that 20% of Situational Awareness is invested in Anthropic where his fiance is chief of staff is also just great entertainment/fodder for the storyline.

I also am intrigued by the setup looking like Anthropic is going to be the last of "the big 3 IPOs". With everyone staring down the onslaught of equity+debt issuance linked to the AI trade, you have to question whether the IPO window is going to stay open for all 3. Are investors going to be full by the time Anthropic comes to market?

I think 2H 2026 is going to be a memorable time in market history.

tweets.text:
haven’t seen anyone comment on this yet but unfortunately vision pro won’t have access to apple’s most powerful on-device models

not even the m5 vision pro, even though its specs are well within the requirements on other devices. maybe there just aren’t enough m5 vision pros in the wild for apple to bother porting it

tweets.text:
Why I'm optimistic about perps

A perpetual is a leveraged bet on price with no expiry. Long or short; pay or collect funding to hold; close whenever. The simplest leverage tool in crypto, and the most used by far. Offshore perp volume topped ~$93T last year.

Why I think it keeps winning:

1. It already won once. BitMEX brought perps to crypto in 2016. Within two years, they overtook dated futures and options. BTC perp volume now runs ~6x spot.

2. First US approvals just landed. Kalshi's BTCPERP went live June 3: the first US-regulated perp. The CFTC cleared Coinbase to route clients into global perps. Kraken's lined up next.

3. The demand is structural. Retail was over half of US options volume last year. Global CFD volume runs past $30T a month. 4B+ people are still unbrokered. That's who perps are built for.

4. Simpler than options. No expiry, no theta decay, no implied vol to track. Exposure is linear, basically spot with leverage. For trades held days, not months, that's the whole appeal.

5. Cleaner than CFDs. CFDs do the same job, but OTC: opaque pricing, broker-set spreads, scattered liquidity. Perps run it on a transparent order book with no bilateral trust.

6. They trade 24/7. Markets move overnight and on weekends—on earnings and headlines, exactly when retail is shut out. Perps don't close.

7. Room to grow is huge. Options, futures, and CFDs do $8T+ a day. Perp DEXs do ~$20B. Catching even 1% is a different-sized business.

Perps started as a crypto workaround and became the better tool for leveraged directional trading. The US cracking the door just widens the funnel.

1. It already won once. BitMEX brought perps to crypto in 2016. Within two years, they overtook dated futures and options. BTC perp volume now runs ~6x spot.

2. First US approvals just landed. Kalshi's BTCPERP went live June 3: the first US-regulated perp. The CFTC cleared Coinbase to route clients into global perps. Kraken's lined up next.

3. The demand is structural. Retail was over half of US options volume last year. Global CFD volume runs past $30T a month. 4B+ people are still unbrokered. That's who perps are built for.

4. Simpler than options. No expiry, no theta decay, no implied vol to track. Exposure is linear, basically spot with leverage. For trades held days, not months, that's the whole appeal.

5. Cleaner than CFDs. CFDs do the same job, but OTC: opaque pricing, broker-set spreads, scattered liquidity. Perps run it on a transparent order book with no bilateral trust.

6. They trade 24/7. Markets move overnight and on weekends—on earnings and headlines, exactly when retail is shut out. Perps don't close.

7. Room to grow is huge. Options, futures, and CFDs do $8T+ a day. Perp DEXs do ~$20B. Catching even 1% is a different-sized business.

Perps started as a crypto workaround and became the better tool for leveraged directional trading. The US cracking the door just widens the funnel.

tweets.text:
$AAPL down ~4%. 

Craig says Siri AI in beta later this year, which explains the sell off.  Investors wanted it in September.  That means the real version is likely early to mid 2027. Funny that the stock actually ticked up 0.5% on the "beta later this year" update given while it's later than what they wanted, it is at least a date that investors can focus on.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:

tweets.text:
$AAPL has sold off 2.6% because the jury is still out on whether Apple can deliver compelling AI.

Yes, the demo is amazing, but Craig didn’t give any timing updates on the new Siri. Most investors (including Gurman) expected it to launch this fall, but Apple provided no comfort that it would actually be ready in September.

That lack of confidence on timing is what is spooking investors. Apple doesn’t seem willing to be on the hook for a specific date, which leaves the bigger question unanswered: Can Apple deliver AI that people actually want?
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: Siri AI is available in English. I did not hear timing. That may be the issue with the stock.

tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
$AAPL announces the new Siri AI and stock trades down 2.5% on the announcement.  

My take: The stock drop is entirely buy on the rumor, sell on the news. Everything they showed is what was expected and the features are something only Apple and Google can do with personalized AI.
tweets.full_text: Siri AI is available in English. I did not hear timing. That may be the issue with the stock.

tweets.text:
$AAPL announces the new Siri AI and stock trades down 2.5% on the announcement.  

My take: The stock drop is entirely buy on the rumor, sell on the news. Everything they showed is what was expected and the features are something only Apple and Google can do with personalized AI.

Keep in mind, it’s still a demo, they overpromised with demos two years ago. 

That said, if they deliver what they showed today, it will drive hardware sales. The reason is the key improvement is personal context and allows more ways to get things done. 

It goes beyond one-shot prompts and can reference previous conversations. The examples felt 10x better than using ChatGPT for personal tasks: checking concert dates, setting a reminder to buy tickets, playing songs from the artist, finding the venue, and getting directions to pick up a friend.

Keep in mind, it’s still a demo, they overpromised with demos two years ago. 

That said, if they deliver what they showed today, it will drive hardware sales. The reason is the key improvement is personal context and allows more ways to get things done. 

It goes beyond one-shot prompts and can reference previous conversations. The examples felt 10x better than using ChatGPT for personal tasks: checking concert dates, setting a reminder to buy tickets, playing songs from the artist, finding the venue, and getting directions to pick up a friend.

tweets.text:
Craig shifts conversation to AI . New Apple Foundation models with Google. Build small models that run on server and on device. 

Craig this will give better:
1l Image underrating and generation. 
dictation, speech. 
2. Personal context understanding that will help you find what you're looking for faster (uses the new Search). 
3. Apple Intelligence can go out to the web and tie it back to on device for more personal context. 

My take: Nice and to be expected. These would not be game changers. Need to see the new Siri which starts now. .
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Trust and safety: Apple spends 10 minutes on new tools for child safety. 

My take: This is the most aggressive I've heard Apple address child safety. They're throwing out the failed Screen Time now have what sounds like a compressive to manage what they can see, who they can

tweets.text:
The credit system functions like a circulatory system that delivers nutrients and buying power to various parts of the economy. A system is considered healthy when borrowed money is used to generate productivity, which in turn produces income. However, it becomes unhealthy when debts and debt service costs increase relative to income. This debt service acts like plaque in the circulatory system, squeezing out spending. Because one individual's debt represents another individual's asset, with holders expecting a good return, increased selling leads to supply and demand issues. When combined with high debt service, it ultimately impairs the system's mechanics.
@edels0n

tweets.text:
Today the new hot narrative on CT about Strategy is that they are "diluting MSTR shareholders".

The only metric that matters to gauge if MSTR shareholders are diluted is the bitcoin-per-share ratio. So far it's up 12.8% this year despite BTC being down.

Some decisions can be dilutive (ie decreasing the BPS) in the short-term, but accretive in the long-term, so you can only judge the result over several months or years.

When Strategy created the USD reserve, it was a short-term hit to the BPS, but maybe it was long-term positive because it enabled them to sell more STRC. (There's no definitive answer, I'm just explaining the dilemma.)

The neutral level that defines whether it's accretive or not to sell MSTR for BTC is around 1.2x EV mNAV, but it's really not a big deal to hit the ATM if it's right below this level, in the sense that it's a drop in the bucket for the bitcoin-per-share, but it's decreasing the leverage of the balance sheet, which *can* be positive.

Selling MSTR shares for USD to top up the USD reserve (which is what all of CT "wanted" Saylor to do in size) is strictly dilutive for the BPS ratio.

(Obviously using $2bn of STRC cash to ape into BTC above $80k three weeks ago to now sell some MSTR shares for USD at a much lower price is a stupid move, but what is done is done.)
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
So Saylor sold MSTR below 1.22x mNAV after saying he wouldn’t, and used the majority of the proceeds to buy more BTC. The rest builds the dividend reserve

heh

tweets.text:
This was always possible anyway by just sending your transactions to a specific validator. Exchanges did this historically with miners to dampen fee volatility. I wouldn’t be surprised they have similar deals in place. 

It only gets problematic if validators also chose not to build in blocks from non-consortium validators.
